speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Today's date is September 7, 1995. The survivor's name is Harry Magid. The interviewer's name is Sarah Aftergood. The city is Beverly Hills, California, USA, and the language will be conducted in English. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 7, 1995. This is Survivors of the Shoah. The survivor is Harry Magid. My name is Sarah Aftergood, the interviewer. We are in Beverly Hills, California. We on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Magid. Tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Harry Magid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthdate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born July 17, 1930, in Poland in a city named Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-P-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Joseph Magid. My mother's name Frida Weingarden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were married in 1929 in Stepan. And a year later, I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in-- we had a flour mill business. And uh, in that-- 1930, the mill burned down. And, uh, then later, he organized with some partners. They rebuilt the mill and oil factory. They used to make oil-- eating-- to eat oil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you start, please, by your earliest recollections of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Uh. When I-- when I was born, my mother's sisters came to us for visit, but I was only a-- a few months old. So I don't remember exactly, but I remember what were talking about. And when she came for visit, and it was during the Depression in 1930. So my father and my mother begged her to take us out to the United States. She promised that she will, but she never did. In 1935, my brother was born, Alex. And I remember when it was the circumcision, and uh, they-- they made a big party and there was a lot of people in our house. And, uh, the-- the Rabbi that circumcised my brother was Levy. He was not only-- he was the rabbi. He was the [INAUDIBLE] in our city, and he was only-- he-- and he was circumcising boys. I was very upset. I was crying. I did-- I did not want that he circumcise my brother. But the end of it was a circumcision, was a lot of cakes and cookies and whiskey, and there was a whole celebration that day. And after that, uh, business were getting better because the Depression was almost over. And then the family-- we had a big house and that was my grandparents' house. And then they built for us a new house in the back of their place. And, uh, I start-- when I was, uh, six years old, I started going to Jewish school. It was the [? Beta ?] [? Sefer, ?] a Hebrew school. We had Jewish schools, then we had non-Jewish schools. Those people who couldn't afford to go to-- because in the Jewish schools, you had to pay. And the non-Jewish schools, it was free from the government, from the Polish government. But I always went to-- to the Jewish school, and I went from '36 to '39. And in 1939, when the war started between Germany and Poland, they split Poland in half. Half-- that part of Poland where we lived, it was the eastern part of Poland that was in-- they called it the Ukraine. They gave it to the Russians. In 1940-- 39, the Russians occupied the part what we lived, and-- and they stayed from 1939 to 1941. Uh, the Jewish population didn't have it so bad over there, but they were looking for-- they were saying that the Jews are anti-communist. They want to send them out to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we had a lot of, uh, friends, and they say, what, to support Jews? Why are you going to send them to Siberia? And it was very fast. In two years, they couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Germans came in in 1941. When the Germans came in in 1941, they told the Ukraine and it says, Jude Kaput. Ukraine, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They-- and they told the Ukrainian they can go to the Jews and take everything away from the Jews. They broke into their homes, the Ukraine. They came from all over the villages, from all over. And they-- with horses and wagons, they took out from the Jewish homes, their furniture. They broke into the stores. They cleaned out everything. And a few months later, in August, they ordered to make a ghetto in Stepan. They took one part of the city. We had the river going by our city, the Horyn. The name of the river was the Horyn. And they made  a eight-foot fence, and they told the Jews they-- they have to get out from their homes and go to the ghetto. So they only could carry what they could take with them. We got into the ghetto, and, uh, some people that had trades were living outside the ghetto, like tailors, shoemakers, uh, uh, plumbers, electricians, so. And the rest of them live in the ghetto. They made separate-- for the women and children separate, for the men separate so they couldn't get together. I think it was for reason not to have children or whatever. And you could only talk to-- to the women or to the men through the fence, but they couldn't get together. We were in the ghetto. They didn't feed the people. They only used to give around 10 dekas of flour a day for each person. And so they made from some of the flour bread, and from some of it they made soup. But the soup was mostly water. It wasn't-- we did not have no salt or sugar, nothing, only water and flour. They stayed, and then they used to take the-- the men to work on the roads. They used to build roads. They was taking all the stones that-- from the Jewish cemetery, and they broke them up. And they-- they told the Jews to build roads with those stones. And, uh, after-- al-- and-- and we had typhus in the-- in the ghetto. A lot of people were dying of typhus, and a lot of people were swelling up. And nobody had any guts to resist anything, because they were like skeletons. And after 13 months, September 22, 1942, they had prepared ditches in the forests. And they took out-- they told the Jews to-- in the ghetto that they taking the Jews to send them to a different city, because this place has to be Juden free. No Jews to-- because they're afraid that the Jews are going to fight against the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they brought over about a 150 wagons with horses, and the children and the older people had to go on the wagons. And the-- and the men had to walk by the wagon. There was-- mostly was Ukrainian police and just a few Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, before that, my father worked in the mill. I came in the day to the mill, and the Ukrainian came over and told my father-- he says, you know, he says, Joseph, I think that they're taking tomorrow out the Jews, and they're going to shoot them in the forest. If I would be you, he says, I would send your son and tell your wife and your other son to go away from the ghetto to hide by some Ukrainian that he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came over to the ghetto, and I told my mother and my brother to- to go to some of our friends that we had that my father-- worked in the mill and he knew them all. And, uh, so-- so my mother put on Ukrainian dress and a big, uh, uh, shawl on her-- on her head. And she asked me, too. She said, why don't you come with us, too? I said, I'll come later.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the evening, when it started to get dark, they surr-- surrounded the ghetto. And they start-- anybody was-- was going around from the ghetto, they were shooting. Next day, they starting in the morning when it-- when it started getting light. They start breaking in the Jewish homes in the ghetto and chasing out all the Jews from the-- from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was hiding. First, I was hiding-- we had a toilet, a big toilet what-- used to go to the ground. It was holes digged out, and so I was hiding under the big toilet. But I couldn't take the smell, so then I went on the attic. I was hiding with another guy from there, from the same house. So came up a Ukrainian, and he caught us there. He was a policeman, but I knew him. And when he caught us, so he says, I'm going to take you down to the wagons. So that guy asking, if I give you a gold watch, will you let us go? He says, yes. So he gave him a gold watch, and he let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I think by 10 minutes later, he sent up a German, and he caught us on the attic. And he took out his gun, and he started beating that-- that older guy because I was that time 12 years old, and I was very small. And he says, Raus, you Schweinhund. And-- and I jump down from the attic, and I ran right away to their wagons, and I jumped on a wagon. When I was on the wagon, uh, I remember now that it was a woman and a daughter. Their name was [? Weiner. ?] The mother was shot in the arm, and she was bleeding very bad. She was screaming. And the daughter was next to her. And then when the-- when the wagon started going out of the city, there was a lot-- by ask wasn't that-- over there was no roads, so there was a lot of dust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figure, anyway they're going to shoot me. I'll try to run away. I ran away one time. They caught me. They brought me back on the wagon. Then we went out a little farther. It was between two stables. I said-- and it was so much dust, you couldn't see nothing. So I say, I'll try to run away again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I managed to run between the two stables, and there was a potato field. And I laid down in the potato field, and I was listening this until all the wagons passed by. Then I figured, I'm gonna go back to Stepan to see maybe my father is-- is in the mill. When I came towards Stepan-- but, uh, five Ukrainian boys that I-- knew them, they caught me. And they say, oh, we going to take him to the police. So one of the boy's mother came out and said, let him go. Let somebody else kill him. Why do you have to-- so they let me go, and I came to the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked the mechanic-- his name was [? Mikhail-- ?] I said, [? Mikhail, ?] where is my father? He says, your father is in the mill underneath the floor. So he let me into the mill, and I-- I went to my, father, and I start talking to my father-- what we going to do? So he says, you know, we can't stay here because when they're gonna come back, the police and the Germans, they're going to come looking for us. So the same night that Mikhail went to one of the Ukrainian-- his name was Philip Szerkow and he took us out from the mill to a farm where he had a big farm. And on the farm, there was stacks of hay. Um, we took on the bottom, and we pulled out hay, and we crawled in underneath the stacks. But before we went, my-- my father says, it's getting cold at-- at night. And we only had summer clothes on, so he picked up about 10 empty flour sacks with us, and we took it with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there hiding more than a week, and then that Philip Szerkow came over and says, you know, I had a message that your wife and your son is about 20 kilometer on the other side of the river in the forest. So me and my father said we have to go and, find uh, mother and my brother. And when we had to go to the river. So at night, we had to cross the river, so my father undressed, and I undressed myself. And he gave me all the clothes, and I put it on my head. And we swim, and I went on my father's, uh, back, and we swim through the river. After that, we came-- we went maybe about 20 kilometer till we found in the forest my mother, my brother, and some other, uh, people from Stepan. We were staying there for about a couple of weeks, and during the day, we used to be in the forests. At night, we used to come into-- to the-- to our friend, the Ukrainian. We used to sleep in the barn. But it was getting colder, and it was raining. And, uh, three policemen caught-- caught us in the forest. They came with dogs and with rifles, and, uh, they say they're going to take us to Gestapo. And over there, the Germans are going to give them for each Jew one kilo salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- But I ran away. They didn't catch me, but they caught my mother, my father, and, uh, two boys from Stepan and two girls from Stepan. After they caught them, so my father says to them, if I'll give you three 10 ruble gold pieces, will you let us go? So they had a conference between themselves, and says, yeah, if you'll give us three 10 ruble gold pieces, we'lll-- we'll let you go. So I had the- the three pieces of gold pieces sewed into my pants. So I hear-- and I run away maybe about a kilometer or maybe a little farther, and I was in the bushes-- and I hear my mother say-- hollers, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]! I recognized my mother's voice, and I, uh, came out. And I say, Mom, what is it? She says, I need the three gold pieces. They're going to let us go. So I gave my mother the three gold pieces, and she went. And she-- she gave it to those three policemen, and they let-- they let everybody go. But when they were walking back, I was in the bushes. I heard they were saying, we'll come tomorrow again. We'll catch him. We'll take them back to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came over, and I told my father that-- that-- what they said, so my father says, we can't stay here because they going to come and they're going to catch us. And they're going to take us. So my father had a friend in another village-- was Komarivka. And he said, we going to go to him. Maybe he's going to help us. He used to be a bootlegger. He used to make whiskey. But he was right by the main road. We came at night, and it was cold and raining. And we stayed in the bushes, and my father went-- it was maybe midnight, or 2 o'clock in the morning-- and he knocked in his window. And he came out, and he talked to my father. And my father told him, listen, maybe you can save us. I mean, after the war, if you're going to survive, we'll give you all we have, and we'll-- And he says, you know, I can't do it, because all the policemens and all the-- they stop by me for whiskey because I'm on the main road. But I'm going to go to my cousin. He lives far in the forest. He's got a farm, maybe-- and he was very poor. He said, maybe he's going to want to hide you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to this cousin, and he talked to his cousin. And, uh, he agreed he's going to hide us as long as we going to pay him. We had a lot of stuff hidden by some Ukrainian, and we had some buried stuff. So when, uh, when he brought us to his cousin, and uh-- it was already winter. There was already snow on the ground, and, um, he had the-- he had the two barns. One was with his, uh, cows and his sheep and his pigs, and one was only with, uh, hay and, uh, straw. So it was get-- getting already very cold. So we took-- we went-- the barn was full of hay in one side, and we took us some hay pulled out, and we crawled in-- in-- in-- in there. And we thought it's gonna be warm, but it was getting very cold. We almost froze there. So my father says, you know, potatoes, when they put them in the ground, don't freeze. So we digged out on the other side where it was the straw a 6 by 6 hole. And we covered that with-- with-- wood, with-- and then we covered it with dirt. And then the straw was on top of it. We made that you could crawl in there, and we crawled in there. And it was very warm in the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- we used to breathe-- it was getting so cold. It-- it was getting below zero, so there used to be icicles hanging, and we used to eat those icicles. We did not have too much food to eat, and we did not have-- even we didn't have enough water. But we were there by him for almost a year. But after we didn't have what to pay him, he says, I don't want to keep you anymore. You have to leave. And my father begged him-- listen, give us a few more days. We'll-- we'll leave-- we'll-- but he says, no. If you don't leave by tomorrow, I'm going to go to police and tell them that Jews are hiding by me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said, it's-- no. We have to leave that night. We were walking and walking and walking. I had-- we did not have shoes or anything. I had rags on my-- on my feet and with strings, uh, so the rags don't fall out, tied up with strings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking till we came to-- to a house, and, uh, it was very late at night, and it was a lot of snow. So my father says, you know, let's get into the barn and in the morning, when, uh, somebody is gonna-- when maybe that guy is gonna come to the barn, I'm going to talk to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got into the barn. We-- we went into the place where he had about five sheeps, and we laid down over there with the sheeps, and it was-- we got warmed up. And in the morning, we hear-- he-- he-- he comes in. He's-- he's hollering, is anybody here? Is anybody here? So my father came out-- he didn't shave himself for a year-- with a big beard, and he says, [INAUDIBLE]. He knew that guy. Do you know who I am? He said, no, I don't who you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Josko. ?] Oh, Dr. [? Josko. ?] I had a dream last night that- that Jews are hiding in my barn. So my father talked to him and said, the war is almost over. And if you can help us and we'll survive, we'll give you all we have. So he agreed. He let us stay there. And, uh, meantime, after we stayed there maybe a week, the Germans and the Ukrainian came and burned down the place that we were hiding at in that place. I don't know how, but somebody must have said that Jews are hiding by that other guy. But they didn't burn down his house, and they didn't burn down his other barn where they had the cows and everything, only the barn that we were hiding. So-- and we were by-- oh, yeah. In the meantime, they announced that they chased out the Germans from Stepan, and they made [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine. That the-- they had their own country now, the Ukraine, and it's going to be-- so they announced that all the Jews that's hiding in the forest can come out. Nobody's gonna kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was some tailors, some, uh, shoemakers. There was a doctor, a dentist. And so they came out, and uh, and they was working in the city. Then they-- one of the Ukrainian-- sent a message to my father-- said, you can come out. You can come and work in the mill. So my father came, and there was maybe about 50 Jews in the city. So my father came out, too, and he was working in the mill. And we had already-- and he was working in the mill, and we had already plenty of food and everything. We even gave plenty food to the people that were helping us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few months, it-- it was going already to 1944, and they already knew that the Russians are not far. So they got together, and the guy that was the-- the leader over there was one of the best friends of my father's. His name was Sacha Krompf. He was the commandant in our city. And he told my father, you can come out. Nobody's gonna touch you. He used to eat in our house. And when it was Poland, the Sacha Krompf was the chief from the fire department, and my father was the assistant chief of the fire department. And, uh, when it broke out-- the war-- in 1941, my-- there was a lot of fires from they were dropping bombs. My father broke his leg, so-- after that-- and after that, so-- um, my father knew Sacha-- Sacha Krompf, and he told him that nobody's gonna touch him. But after a few months, they found out that the Russians are coming. They got together and said they have to kill all the Jews.
